next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The line was: *I will complete my assignments on time.*

By Monday afternoon, one of the consequences had caught up with him in Herbology.

Gavin wrote it for the fifteenth time, and the word *complete* came out looking like *conplete* because his hand was doing the thing again, the fine tremor in his left fingers that had been with him since November. He pressed harder and the quill dug a shallow trench through the parchment. The ink bled sideways into the tear.

He crossed it out and started the sixteenth line.

Professor Sprout was at her desk at the front of the room, marking third-year essays with the stub of a quill she'd broken that morning on a Venomous Tentacula pot that needed splitting. She held it like a pencil sharpened to its last inch, her thick fingers cramped around it, earth worked permanently into the creases of her knuckles. A pot of something small and yellow-green sat at the corner of her desk — she kept turning it a quarter-rotation every few minutes, chasing the afternoon light that came through the greenhouse-adjacent windows. Her attention had stayed on the essays for twenty minutes straight. The red annotations in the margins were detailed, careful, twice the length of anything the students had probably written themselves. Gavin suspected it was the sort of habit that cost her hours, and that she kept doing it for the same reason she still watered the Greenhouse Three seedlings every morning even though no one was taking N.E.W.T. Herbology this year. That, he thought, was how you got through a year like this: keep doing the old jobs...
open_hooks_to_carry: The wards visible: vast, interlocking structures perceived through substrate-awareness. Beautiful and failing. Thin places visible as areas of degradation
The entity: rendered precisely as a condition, not a creature. The analogy of a rising water table — not intention, not threat, just incompatibility at a scale that makes malice irrelevant
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ramatic, the kind of thing you did for a person who was hurting when you had no cure. She was good at it. She had been good at it all year, at the small acts of presence that did not fix anything but meant that the unfixed thing was witnessed. She was good at this and Gavin was not good at this and the difference between them was not a matter of talent but of where they were each pointing their attention.

Gavin went upstairs. He sat on his bed and opened the manuscript to the coupling problem and worked on it. After ten minutes his nose started bleeding again, a slow seep that he caught with his handkerchief, tilting his head forward so the blood fell on the cloth and not on the pages. He kept working. The notation shifted under his attention, configurations resolving and collapsing, the logarithmic differential still resisting his attempts to frame it in terms his mind could process.

In the common room below, one floor down and a spiral staircase away, Miren sat with Duncan by the fire. Gavin was on his bed with the manuscript, blood on his handkerchief, the ward-substrate coupling problem absorbing what the guilt had not. These things happened in the same building, on the same night, and the gap between them felt structural, as real to Gavin as the substrate itself: one floor of stairs and something else he had no measurement for.

He worked until his vision blurred and the notation dissolved into shapes he could not read. Then he lay back on his bed with the manuscript on his chest and his robes still on and the bloody handkerchief balled in his fist and did not sleep.
state_deltas_required: Direct perception of the cosmic threat — Gavin can now see the wards, see where they are thin, and sense what is beyond them. The threat has moved from translated description to lived experience

The cosmic threat becomes personal. Gavin perceives the wards from the Astronomy Tower and senses the entity beyond them directly. The grief is not for himself but for a world that does not know it is endangered
